points: An electric cell is a portable source of electrical energy with two terminals — positive (+ve, protruding metal cap) and negative (−ve, flat metal disc); a battery is a combination of two or more cells connected in series (positive terminal of one cell to negative of the next).

An incandescent lamp has a thin filament that heats up and glows when current passes through it; it has no polarity — works either way in a circuit; a fused lamp has a broken filament that stops current flow.

An LED (Light Emitting Diode) has no filament; its longer wire is positive and shorter wire is negative; current flows through it in only one direction — it glows only when connected with the correct polarity (positive of LED to positive of battery).

summary.category: Electrical Circuits, Switches, and Circuit Diagrams
points: An electrical circuit is a complete closed path for electric current; current flows from the positive to the negative terminal of the cell; a break anywhere creates an open circuit and stops current flow, making the lamp go out.
A switch is a device that completes (ON — closed circuit, lamp glows) or breaks (OFF — open circuit, lamp does not glow) a circuit; it can be placed anywhere in the circuit.

A circuit diagram represents an electrical circuit using standard symbols (defined by IEC, ANSI, IEEE); in the cell symbol, the long line is the positive terminal and the short line is the negative terminal.

Conductors are materials through which electric current flows easily — most metals are conductors; silver, copper, and gold are the best conductors, with copper most used for wires due to its lower cost and availability.

Insulators (plastic, rubber, ceramics, glass, wood) do not allow current to flow; wires are made of copper (conductor) covered with plastic or rubber (insulator) to prevent electric shocks and short circuits.

Electricity from cells/batteries is Direct Current (DC); electricity from power plants via wall sockets is Alternating Current (AC); the human body is a conductor — never touch switches with wet hands, use damaged equipment, or experiment with mains electricity.
